author: FP&A Analyst
comment: An unplanned client project was won in late March and delivered inside April; external production was booked at short-notice premium rates. Together with the matching revenue line the net margin impact is slightly negative. Change-order discipline flagged to the project director.

author: FP&A Analyst
comment: Unbudgeted revenue from the same late-won April project; see the matching COGS variance. Recognized on delivery.

author: FP&A Analyst
comment: November events were delivered with in-house crew and staging stock instead of the subcontracting assumed in budget; Q4 freight was also renegotiated. Genuine savings, not a timing shift.

author: FP&A Analyst
comment: A client brought part of its September roadshow forward into July. Timing between months rather than new business; the September shortfall stayed within tolerance.

author: FP&A Analyst
comment: Two platform go-lives slipped from January into February on client-side content delays. Revenue is recognized on delivery, so January carries the gap; both projects were invoiced in February.

author: FP&A Analyst
comment: A brand activation sold for early 2025 was pulled into October at the client's request. The matching cost variance appears on the COGS line the same month.

author: FP&A Analyst
comment: Production costs of the pulled-forward October activation; see the matching revenue variance.

author: FP&A Analyst
comment: External development spend paused over the year-end delivery freeze and resumed in February. This recurs every holiday season; budget phasing to be corrected in the next cycle.

author: FP&A Analyst
comment: One retainer client commissioned an out-of-cycle campaign microsite in an otherwise quiet August.

author: FP&A Analyst
comment: Two institutional clients pushed their October milestone invoicing into November after a budget-cycle approval delay on their side. Timing only.

author: FP&A Analyst
comment: Contractor spend deferred during the summer staffing gap on the delivery bench; caught up from September.

author: FP&A Analyst
comment: June delivery mix skewed toward license resale rather than custom build, which carries lower external cost.

author: FP&A Analyst
comment: The June campaign reused spring creative assets instead of commissioning a new shoot.

author: FP&A Analyst
comment: Print and media rates increased at the main supplier from March; a rate-card renegotiation is underway.
